title: Plug-in hybrid vs full EV vs petrol: running cost compared
description: Real per-mile and annual running costs for plug-in hybrids, full EVs and petrol cars in the UK, using mid-2026 fuel prices and the Ofgem cap.
blurb: Per-mile and annual costs for PHEV, full EV and petrol, with the one habit that decides which wins.
lede: Choosing between a plug-in hybrid, a full electric car and sticking with petrol looks like a three-way fuel price comparison, but it is really a question about your own habits. A full EV charged overnight at home costs around 2p a mile. The same EV charged only at public rapid chargers costs more per mile than petrol. And a plug-in hybrid sits anywhere between the cheapest and the most expensive option on this page depending entirely on whether you plug it in every night or treat it as a normal petrol car with a heavy battery. This guide puts real mid-2026 numbers on all three so you can see which one fits the way you actually drive and charge.
short_answer: At mid-2026 prices, a full EV charged on an off-peak home tariff costs roughly 2p a mile, against about 16p a mile for a 45 mpg petrol car, so a typical 8,000-mile year costs around £160 versus £1,285. A plug-in hybrid charged nightly and driven half its miles on electricity lands near 10p a mile, but an unplugged PHEV costs slightly more than the equivalent petrol car. The deciding factor is home charging: with a driveway and a cheap EV tariff the full EV wins comfortably; without one, the gap shrinks fast.

sections.html:
The three options buy their miles in different currencies, so the comparison only makes sense per mile.

  • Petrol car: every mile is bought at the pump. At around 159p a litre (mid 2026), a realistic 45 mpg works out at about 16.1p a mile. There is no cheap rate and no way to shift the cost around, although driving style still matters, as our hypermiling guide covers.
  • Full EV: every mile is bought in kilowatt hours, and the price of a kilowatt hour varies enormously. A typical EV does about 3.5 miles per kWh, so the cost per mile runs from 2p on an off-peak home tariff to over 22p at a public rapid charger. Where and when you charge matters more than which car you drive.
  • Plug-in hybrid (PHEV): a petrol car with a small battery, usually good for 25 to 50 miles of electric range. Miles driven on the battery cost EV money; miles driven on the engine cost petrol money, often at a slightly worse mpg than a comparable pure petrol car because the PHEV carries 200 to 300 kg of extra hardware.

That last point is the crux of the whole comparison. A PHEV is two cars in one body, and its running cost depends on which of the two you actually use. Official WLTP fuel economy figures for PHEVs (often 150 mpg or more) assume the battery is charged constantly and are best ignored for budgeting.

sections.h2: Cost per mile: the core numbers
sections.html:
Here are the per-mile costs at mid-2026 prices, assuming a full EV at 3.5 miles per kWh, PHEV electric running at 3.0 miles per kWh (PHEVs are less efficient on battery than purpose-built EVs), PHEV petrol mode at 42 mpg and a comparable petrol car at 45 mpg.

How the mile is poweredRate usedCost per mile
Full EV, off-peak home charging7p per kWh2.0p
PHEV on battery, off-peak home charging7p per kWh2.3p
Full EV, standard home rate26.11p per kWh7.5p
PHEV on battery, standard home rate26.11p per kWh8.7p
Petrol car, 45 mpg159p per litre16.1p
PHEV on engine, 42 mpg159p per litre17.2p
Full EV, public rapid charging79p per kWh22.6p

Electricity at 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026; off-peak EV tariffs typically 7 to 9p per kWh; public rapid average around 79p per kWh (Zapmap, mid 2026); petrol around 159p per litre (mid 2026).

Two things jump out. First, the cheapest and most expensive rows are both electric, more than a tenfold spread, which is why home charging cost dominates any EV sums. Second, a PHEV running on its engine costs slightly more per mile than the petrol car it replaced. The hybrid only earns its keep when the battery is doing the work. To rerun these numbers with your own car's efficiency, see miles per kWh explained.

sections.h2: What that means over a year
sections.html:
Multiply those per-mile figures across a typical 8,000-mile year and the gaps become real money.

Scenario, 8,000 miles a yearAnnual fuel or charging cost
Full EV, charged overnight on an off-peak tariff£160
Full EV, charged at the standard cap rate£597
PHEV, plugged in nightly, half its miles on battery (off-peak)£780
Petrol car, 45 mpg£1,285
PHEV, never plugged in, 42 mpg£1,377
Full EV, relying mainly on public rapid chargers£1,806

At 26.11p per kWh (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026), 7p per kWh off-peak, 79p per kWh public rapid and 159p per litre petrol, mid 2026.

Read from the top: the off-peak EV saves over £1,100 a year against petrol. Even charging at the full capped rate, the EV still saves nearly £700. The well-used PHEV saves around £500. But the bottom two rows are the cautionary tales. A PHEV that never sees a plug costs about £90 a year more than the simpler petrol car, and an EV that lives on rapid chargers costs over £500 a year more than petrol. For a deeper petrol comparison with different mileages, see EV running cost vs petrol.

sections.h2: The PHEV catch: it only wins if you plug it in every night
sections.html:
Plug-in hybrids have the widest spread of real-world outcomes of any car type, and the spread comes down to one habit.

A PHEV with 35 miles of real electric range, charged every night, covers the school run, the commute and the shopping entirely on cheap electricity, and only burns petrol on longer trips. For a driver whose daily mileage fits inside the battery, 60 to 80 per cent of annual miles can be electric, and the running cost gets close to a full EV's.

The same car, never plugged in, hauls its battery around as dead weight and returns worse fuel economy than the petrol equivalent. Fleet studies in the UK and Europe have repeatedly found that real-world PHEV fuel consumption averages two to three times the official figure, mostly because company-car drivers with fuel cards had no incentive to charge. If that describes how you would use one, a PHEV is the worst of the three options, not a compromise between them.

Honest questions to ask before choosing a PHEV:

  • Can you charge at home? A three-pin socket is enough for a PHEV's small battery, refilling overnight without a dedicated charger.
  • Is your typical daily mileage under the car's real electric range (knock 20 to 30 per cent off the advertised figure, more in winter)?
  • Will you actually plug it in every night, every season? Be honest. It is a small chore that decides hundreds of pounds a year.
  • Do you make regular trips beyond EV-comfortable range, towing, or rural driving where rapid chargers are sparse? These are the cases where the PHEV's petrol engine genuinely earns its place.

We cover the day-to-day numbers in more detail in our plug-in hybrid running cost guide.

sections.h2: Beyond fuel: tax, servicing and the other costs
sections.html:
Fuel is the biggest gap between the three, but not the only one.

  • Road tax (VED): since April 2025, EVs and PHEVs pay standard-rate VED like petrol cars, and the expensive car supplement applies to new cars over £40,000, which catches many EVs. The headline tax advantage has largely gone, so check current rates on the GOV.UK vehicle tax pages before you buy.
  • Servicing: full EVs have no oil, no clutch, no exhaust and far fewer moving parts, and regenerative braking spares the pads. Servicing typically runs 20 to 40 per cent cheaper than petrol. A PHEV has both drivetrains to maintain, so it sits at the petrol end of the servicing scale, sometimes above it.
  • Insurance: EVs have historically cost a little more to insure, though the gap has been closing as repair networks mature. Quote both before deciding rather than assuming.
  • Depreciation: the largest cost of owning any newer car, and the hardest to predict. Used EV prices fell sharply in 2023 and 2024, which hurt early owners but now makes used EVs some of the cheapest motoring available per mile.
  • Home charger: a full EV benefits from a 7 kW wallbox, typically £800 to £1,100 installed. A PHEV usually does not need one.

If you want the full picture including purchase price and depreciation, our EV total cost of ownership guide walks through it.

sections.h2: Which one fits you: the bottom line
sections.html:
The running cost ranking is not fixed; it follows your charging access and mileage pattern.

  • Driveway plus an EV tariff: full EV wins, and it is not close. At around 2p a mile on a 7p overnight rate, a typical year costs less than two tanks of petrol. Picking the right tariff is most of the win, so start with how EV electricity tariffs work and schedule charging for the cheap overnight window.
  • Driveway, but regular long unpredictable trips: a PHEV charged nightly is a strong second. You get near-EV costs for daily driving and zero range anxiety for the rest, at the price of maintaining two drivetrains.
  • No home charging: run the numbers carefully before leaving petrol. Public rapid charging at around 79p per kWh costs more per mile than petrol, and slower public chargers at around 54p roughly match it. Workplace charging, kerbside lamp-post chargers or a charger-equipped supermarket habit can still tilt the maths toward an EV, but it takes planning. See charging an EV without a driveway.
  • A PHEV you will not plug in is the worst buy on this page. It costs more than petrol to fuel, more to service and more to buy. If the nightly plug-in is not going to happen, choose a full EV or an efficient petrol or conventional hybrid instead.

Whichever way you lean, do the sums with your own mileage and your own pence-per-kWh rate rather than headline figures. The method takes five minutes and the answer is often different from the one the showroom suggests.

faq.q: Is a plug-in hybrid cheaper to run than a petrol car?
faq.a: Only if you charge it regularly. Plugged in nightly and driven mostly within its electric range, a PHEV can cost roughly half as much per mile as petrol. Never plugged in, it returns slightly worse fuel economy than the equivalent petrol car because of the extra weight, so it costs more to run, not less.

faq.q: How much cheaper is a full EV than petrol per mile?
faq.a: Charged at home overnight on an off-peak EV tariff of around 7p per kWh, a typical EV costs about 2p a mile against roughly 16p a mile for a 45 mpg petrol car. At the standard capped rate of 26.11p per kWh (July to September 2026) the EV costs about 7.5p a mile, still less than half the petrol figure.

faq.q: Is an EV still cheaper if I can only use public chargers?
faq.a: Usually not at rapid chargers. At around 79p per kWh (mid 2026), a rapid-charged EV costs about 22.6p a mile, more than petrol. Slower public chargers at around 54p per kWh roughly match petrol. Cheaper kerbside, workplace or supermarket charging can still make an EV work without a driveway, but it needs planning.

faq.q: Do electric cars and plug-in hybrids pay road tax?
faq.a: Yes. Since April 2025, EVs and PHEVs pay standard-rate vehicle excise duty like petrol cars, and the expensive car supplement applies to new cars with a list price over £40,000. Check the current rates on GOV.UK, as they change with each tax year.

faq.q: Does a plug-in hybrid need a home wallbox charger?
faq.a: No. PHEV batteries are small, typically 10 to 20 kWh, so a standard three-pin socket refills one overnight. A dedicated 7 kW wallbox is worthwhile for a full EV but is optional for a PHEV, which removes £800 to £1,100 of installation cost from the comparison.
